question: Has the national fatal police shooting rate changed from 2015-2024?
method: OLS: national fatal_shootings_per_1m ~ year_index
direction: +0.0503 fatal shootings per 1M residents per year; 95% CI [+0.0250, +0.0756]
conclusion: National rate shows a statistically significant upward annual trend in the simple national time series.
caution: Only 10 annual observations; treat as descriptive trend evidence, not causal.

question: Is the national fatal shooting rate monotonic over time?
method: Spearman rank correlation: year vs national rate per 1M
direction: Positive means later years tend to have higher rates.
conclusion: National rate is significantly monotonic upward over the decade.
caution: Ranks only; ignores population exposure model and serial dependence.

question: Has the national unarmed fatal shooting rate changed from 2015-2024?
method: OLS: national unarmed_per_1m ~ year_index
direction: -0.0165 unarmed fatal shootings per 1M residents per year; 95% CI [-0.0247, -0.0083]
conclusion: National unarmed rate shows a statistically significant downward annual trend.
caution: Unarmed coding can be sensitive to reporting/categorization changes.

question: Is the national unarmed rate monotonic over time?
method: Spearman rank correlation: year vs national unarmed rate per 1M
direction: Negative means later years tend to have lower unarmed rates.
conclusion: National unarmed rate is significantly monotonic downward.
caution: Ranks only; counts are small and annual coding practices matter.

question: Does the state-year count rate change over time after controlling for state?
method: Poisson GLM with log(population) offset, state fixed effects, cluster-robust SE by state
direction: IRR/year=1.0157; 95% CI [0.9985, 1.0332]; Pearson overdispersion=1.21
conclusion: State fixed-effects Poisson trend is upward but not significant after clustered SE and FDR correction.
caution: Cluster-robust Poisson reduces but does not eliminate all panel-dependence concerns.

question: Is there a post-2020 shift in rates after controlling for linear trend and state?
method: Poisson GLM: count ~ year_index + post_2020 + state fixed effects + log(population) offset
conclusion: No statistically reliable post-2020 step change after adjusting for state and linear trend.
caution: A simple step-change model; not a causal test and may absorb nonlinear trend into post period.

question: Are 2015-2024 state counts consistent with one common national per-capita rate?
method: Chi-square goodness-of-fit: observed state counts vs expected counts proportional to population-year exposure
direction: Large chi-square means state counts vary far more than population alone would predict. Top positive residuals: NM, AZ, CO, OK, AK; top negative residuals: NY, MA, NJ, IL, PA.
conclusion: State totals are not explainable by population exposure alone; geography matters substantially.
caution: This confirms geographic heterogeneity; it does not explain why states differ.

question: Do state annualized fatal-shooting rates differ by Census region?
method: Kruskal-Wallis test on state annualized rates by region
direction: Higher median rates by region: West: 5.56/1M; South: 3.99/1M; Midwest: 2.69/1M; Northeast: 1.19/1M
conclusion: Census regions differ significantly in state annualized rates; West is highest by median rate.
caution: State rates within regions are not independent cultural/legal systems; this is exploratory.

question: Did the share of fatal shootings with body camera indicated vary by year?
method: Chi-square test of independence: year x body_camera
direction: Body-camera indicated share ranged from 7.5% to 30.3% across years.
conclusion: Body-camera indication varies significantly by year; association is modest.
caution: This is incident-level reporting association, not per-capita rate or causality.

question: Within recorded fatal shootings with known race, does unarmed share vary by race/ethnicity label?
method: Chi-square test of independence: collapsed race/ethnicity label x is_unarmed
direction: Unarmed share among fatal incidents: Other/multiracial: 10.0%; Black: 7.2%; Native American: 6.8%; Hispanic: 6.5%; Asian heritage: 4.9%; White: 4.8%
conclusion: Unarmed share differs by recorded race/ethnicity label, but effect size is very small; do not read as per-capita racial risk.
caution: This is not racial per-capita risk because group population denominators were not in the current package.

question: Is mental-illness-related indication associated with whether the decedent was unarmed?
method: Chi-square test of independence: mental_illness_related x is_unarmed
direction: Unarmed share: not mental-illness-related=5.5%, mental-illness-related=5.1%.
conclusion: No meaningful association between mental-illness-related flag and unarmed status in this dataset.
caution: Incident-level association; coding/reporting may vary across agencies and time.

question: Is the best ensemble model biased on the 2023-2024 holdout?
method: One-sample t-test of prediction error (predicted - actual) against 0
direction: Mean error=-1.08 fatal shootings; median error=-0.49. Negative means underprediction.
conclusion: Mean ensemble residual is slightly negative but not statistically distinguishable from zero.
caution: State-year residuals are not guaranteed independent; use with holdout context.

question: Is the ensemble residual distribution approximately normal?
method: Shapiro-Wilk normality test on ensemble holdout residuals
direction: Small p-value means residuals deviate from a normal bell curve.
conclusion: Ensemble residuals are non-normal; prefer robust/nonparametric intervals over normal-theory assumptions.
caution: Normality is not required for prediction usefulness; it matters for parametric uncertainty statements.

question: Is the median ensemble prediction error different from zero?
method: Wilcoxon signed-rank test of residuals against zero
direction: Median error=-0.49; negative means underprediction.
conclusion: Median ensemble residual is not statistically distinguishable from zero.
caution: Complements the mean-bias t-test.

question: Does the ensemble have lower absolute holdout error than lag1_baseline?
method: Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on absolute errors across 2023-2024 state-year holdout rows
direction: Ensemble MAE=4.04; lag1_baseline MAE=4.22; difference=-0.18. Negative favors ensemble.
conclusion: Ensemble does not significantly outperform this comparator on paired absolute holdout errors.
caution: Tests predictive error only on the 2023-2024 holdout; future years may differ.

question: Does the ensemble have lower absolute holdout error than roll3_baseline?
method: Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on absolute errors across 2023-2024 state-year holdout rows
direction: Ensemble MAE=4.04; roll3_baseline MAE=4.12; difference=-0.08. Negative favors ensemble.
conclusion: Ensemble does not significantly outperform this comparator on paired absolute holdout errors.
caution: Tests predictive error only on the 2023-2024 holdout; future years may differ.

question: Does the ensemble have lower absolute holdout error than prev_national_rate_baseline?
method: Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on absolute errors across 2023-2024 state-year holdout rows
direction: Ensemble MAE=4.04; prev_national_rate_baseline MAE=8.19; difference=-4.16. Negative favors ensemble.
conclusion: Ensemble has significantly lower absolute holdout error than this comparator.
caution: Tests predictive error only on the 2023-2024 holdout; future years may differ.

question: Does the ensemble have lower absolute holdout error than random_forest_enhanced_lagged?
method: Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on absolute errors across 2023-2024 state-year holdout rows
direction: Ensemble MAE=4.04; random_forest_enhanced_lagged MAE=4.68; difference=-0.65. Negative favors ensemble.
conclusion: Ensemble has significantly lower absolute holdout error than this comparator.
caution: Tests predictive error only on the 2023-2024 holdout; future years may differ.

question: Does the ensemble have lower absolute holdout error than ridge_enhanced_lagged?
method: Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on absolute errors across 2023-2024 state-year holdout rows
direction: Ensemble MAE=4.04; ridge_enhanced_lagged MAE=4.72; difference=-0.68. Negative favors ensemble.
conclusion: Ensemble has significantly lower absolute holdout error than this comparator.
caution: Tests predictive error only on the 2023-2024 holdout; future years may differ.

question: Does the ensemble have lower absolute holdout error than poisson_enhanced_lagged?
method: Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on absolute errors across 2023-2024 state-year holdout rows
direction: Ensemble MAE=4.04; poisson_enhanced_lagged MAE=5.07; difference=-1.03. Negative favors ensemble.
conclusion: Ensemble has significantly lower absolute holdout error than this comparator.
caution: Tests predictive error only on the 2023-2024 holdout; future years may differ.

question: Across states, do higher overall annualized rates correspond to higher unarmed annualized rates?
method: Pearson correlation between state annualized overall fatal-shooting rate and state annualized unarmed rate
direction: Pearson r=0.678; Spearman rho=0.583 (p=7.02e-06).
conclusion: States with higher overall rates also tend to have higher unarmed rates, but this is partly mechanical because unarmed is a subset of total.
caution: Unarmed fatal shootings are a subset of total fatal shootings, so some association is mathematically expected; interpret as descriptive.
